params.text:
Why the clock matters

Most medicines have some flexibility around the scheduled time. A small number do not. Time-critical medicines have to be given within a narrow window because the person's body depends on a steady level of the medicine, and that level drops away quickly. For these medicines, the person's own timings come first — not the order of your round.

  • Parkinson's disease medicines: the clearest example, and the reason for the Get It On Time principle. A late or missed dose can leave someone unable to move, stand, swallow or speak. The effects can be serious and long-lasting, and may not settle quickly once the dose is finally given. Many people take their medicines at unusual times that look wrong on a rota but are exactly right for them.
  • Insulin: timing is tied to meals and to the person's blood glucose, so giving it early, late or without the meal changes the effect.
  • Epilepsy medicines: steady levels help keep seizures controlled; gaps in doses increase the risk of a seizure.
  • Myasthenia gravis medicines: these control muscle weakness that can affect swallowing and breathing, so late doses can leave someone very unwell.

Saying you will give it at the next call is not acceptable for any of these. Doubling up later does not undo the gap, and combining doses is never your decision. If a person's care plan flags a medicine as time-critical, treat that call as fixed.

If you are running late or a dose has been missed

  • Ring the office or your manager immediately: do this as soon as you know you will be late, not after the visit. There may be time to send another worker or to get advice before the window closes.
  • Get advice from the right person: the pharmacist, GP or NHS 111 can advise on a missed or delayed dose. If the person is unwell — struggling to breathe, unable to swallow, unresponsive or having a seizure — call 999.
  • Never guess, double up or catch up: do not give two doses together, and do not shift later doses to make the day work. Only a prescriber or pharmacist can advise on that.
  • Record it accurately: use the correct MAR chart code, write what happened and when, note who you contacted and what advice you were given.

Some medicines carry extra risk whenever they go wrong, not only when they are late. Anticoagulants, insulin, opioid painkillers and sedatives all need particular care with identity, dose and timing.

A missed or late dose is an incident. It gets reported, recorded and reviewed — not quietly absorbed into the next call.

params.question: You are 45 minutes behind and your next call is a person whose Parkinson's medicine is due now. What is the right action?

answers.text: Phone the office as soon as you realise you will be late, so cover can be arranged or advice sought, then record what happened and any advice given.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Raising it early is what gives the service a chance to protect the dose. Parkinson's medicines are time-critical and a late dose can have serious effects.

answers.text: Carry on and give the dose when you arrive, then shift the rest of the day's doses back to keep the gaps even.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Adjusting a medicines schedule is a prescriber's decision, not a care worker's. You must seek advice rather than rework the timings yourself.

answers.text: Skip the dose and give a double dose at the next call so the person does not miss out.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Never double up doses. This is unsafe and outside your role, and it does not undo the missed window.

answers.text: Say nothing, as being under an hour late is within normal tolerance for a care round.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Time-critical medicines do not have that tolerance, and a missed or late dose is an incident that must be reported and recorded.
